coredata.dc:title: Mussel-inspired preparation of temperature-responsive polymer brushes modified layered double hydroxides@Pd/carbon dots hybrid for catalytic applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yu
Zhu, Wenjing
Cui, Donghui
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: A novel type of multicomponent nanocomposite of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/carbon dots (CDs) was successfully constructed for highly efficient heterogeneous catalytic reduction from two-dimensional layer double hydroxide (LDH) nanosheet functionalized with thermo-responsive PNIPAM polymer brushes based on the mussel-inspired chemistry. CDs were used as a reductant and stabilizer by in situ loading metal Pd onto LDH@PDA@PNIPAM. Compared with LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd catalyst without CDs modification, the designed LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanohybrid catalyst had better catalytic reduction ability for various dyes and nitrophenol thanks to the synergistic catalytic effect between metal active center and CDs, LDH and their high dispersion stability. Especially, a good catalytic reduction activity was demonstrated for methylene blue (MB) with a high TOF (545.2 min−1). Furthermore, the nanohybrid presented interesting thermo-responsive catalytic property due to the existence of PNIPAM polymer chains. In addition, the prepared catalyst also exhibited outstanding catalytic property for the Knoevenagel condensation reaction. Our study provides a new way for the composite preparation of organic-inorganic hybrid materials by polymer and inorganic materials, and shows great potential in the field of catalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mussel-inspired chemistry
Layer double hydroxide
Pd nanoparticles
Thermosensitive polymer
Catalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst for dyes
Catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst for 4-NP
Catalytic performance of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs for Knoevenagel condensation reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (a, b), high-resolution TEM image (c) and Pd NP size distribution (f) of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs; TEM images (d, e) of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd, Inset: Pd NP size distribution of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd.
FTIR spectra of different samples: (a) PNIPAM-SH, (b) LDH, (c) LDH@PDA, (d) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM, (e) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs, and (f) CDs.
XRD patterns of different samples: (a) LDH, (b) LDH@PDA, (c) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM, (d) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs, and (e) CDs.
(a) XPS spectra of a broad scan survey of different samples. XPS spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, and Pd 3d peaks of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs (b-d).
High-resolution XPS spectra of different samples: (a) Mg 2p, (b) Al 2p, (c) C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) N 1 s, and (f) S 2p.
TGA curves of different samples under N2 atmosphere: (a) LDH, (b) LDH@PDA, (c) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM, (d) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs, and (e) CDs.

Successive UV–vis spectra for MB catalytic reduction using different catalysts (20 μL, 0.05 mg mL−1): (a) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs, (b) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd, (c) LDH@Pd/CDs. (d) Plots of ln(Ct/C0) versus reaction time (t) and the value of correlation coefficient (R2) for the MB reduction using different nanocatalysts (Pd concentration: 1.83 × 10−7 M; MB: 2.0 mL, 0.013 mM; NaBH4: 1.0 mL, 0.5 M).
Schematic illustration of catalytic reduction mechanism of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@ Pd/CDs for MB and 4-NP.

Successive UV–Vis absorption spectra of different catalysts for 4-NP: (a) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs (40 μL, 0.2 mg mL−1), (b) LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd, (c) LDH@Pd/CDs. (d) Plots of ln(Ct/C0) versus reaction time (t) and the value of correlation coefficient (R2) for the 4-NP reduction using different nanocatalysts (Pd concentration: 1.47 × 10−6 M, 2.0 mL 0.16 mM 4-NP, 1.0 mL 0.2 M NaBH4).

(a) Plots of ln(Ct/C0) versus reaction time (t) at different temperatures. (b) The dependence of the apparent rate constant (k) on temperature and the value of correlation coefficient (R2) for the reduction of 4-NP with LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs. Reaction conditions: 2.0 mL 0.16 mM 4-NP, 40 μL, 0.2 mg mL−1 catalyst and 1.0 mL 0.2 M NaBH4.
Schematic synthesis of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd nanohybrid catalysts.
The content of each element in different samples, and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst before and after the catalytic cycle.
Catalytic performance of different catalysts for MB.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The calculation for TOF values of the catalytic reduction of 4-NP at 100%.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction condition: a benzaldehyde (1.0 mmol), malononitrile (1.2 mmol), water (5.0 mL), 60 °C, 6 h. b Room temperature, 30 min, 2.0 mmol NaBH4.c Based on 1H NMR analysis.

head.ce:title: Mussel-inspired preparation of temperature-responsive polymer brushes modified layered double hydroxides@Pd/carbon dots hybrid for catalytic applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Element Education Department, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Element Education Department, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Yang and W. Zhu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel type of multicomponent nanocomposite of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/carbon dots (CDs) was successfully constructed for highly efficient heterogeneous catalytic reduction from two-dimensional layer double hydroxide (LDH) nanosheet functionalized with thermo-responsive PNIPAM polymer brushes based on the mussel-inspired chemistry. CDs were used as a reductant and stabilizer by in situ loading metal Pd onto LDH@PDA@PNIPAM. Compared with LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd catalyst without CDs modification, the designed LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanohybrid catalyst had better catalytic reduction ability for various dyes and nitrophenol thanks to the synergistic catalytic effect between metal active center and CDs, LDH and their high dispersion stability. Especially, a good catalytic reduction activity was demonstrated for methylene blue (MB) with a high TOF (545.2 min−1). Furthermore, the nanohybrid presented interesting thermo-responsive catalytic property due to the existence of PNIPAM polymer chains. In addition, the prepared catalyst also exhibited outstanding catalytic property for the Knoevenagel condensation reaction. Our study provides a new way for the composite preparation of organic-inorganic hybrid materials by polymer and inorganic materials, and shows great potential in the field of catalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanoparticles have features of small size and large specific surface area, and show broad application prospects in the fields of organic catalysis, environmental pollutant treatment, and biological imaging and detection (Wang et al., 2016). However, nanoparticles are easy to aggregate and unstable, which seriously affects their catalytic activity and practical application. Therefore, preventing the aggregation of metal nanoparticles (MNPs) is the key to improve the catalytic activity of catalysts. At present, a lot of researches have loaded metal nanoparticles on different organic and inorganic solid supports, which have been widely used in diverse fields such as catalysis, electrochemistry, and water treatment (Narayan et al., 2019). The support of the catalyst not only dispersed the metal catalytic components, but also played an imperative role in controlling the dispersion and particle size of the catalyst, which affected the catalytic performance through the synergy between different components. Therefore, it is imperative to employ the catalyst support to improve the stability and catalytic efficiency of the metal catalyst (Liao et al., 2019).

Layered double hydroxide (LDH) have a broad applications in the fields of catalytic, biological, magnetic, and optical functional materials due to their unique structural properties and composition(Gunjakar et al., 2014; Li et al., 2014a). The main structure of LDH is generally composed of hydroxides of two metals and has unique properties such as exchangeable anions, adjustable cations and active sites on the surface, which is usually an ideal precursor for preparing nanocatalysts with specific morphology and surface structure (Xu and Wei, 2018). In addition, LDH can also be used as supports for metal nanoparticles, which not only prevented metal nanoparticles from aggregating through the external constraints of the LDH layer, but also promoted the rapid diffusion of reactants to the catalyst's surface (Climent et al., 2004; Fang et al., 2011). Furthermore, the LDH host layer could provide base sites with enhanced synergistic effects for supported metal nanoparticles (Li et al., 2014a). By controlling the particle size, composition and morphology of the catalyst, or adjusting the surface/interface defect and electronic structure of the catalyst, the active sites of LDH can be effectively adjusted (Xu and Wei, 2018). Therefore, the main layer itself not only served as a support of metal catalysts, but also played a role in assisting synergistic catalysis in the catalytic reaction (Zhang et al., 2019). Zhang et al. reported the ultrafine Au nanoclusters supported by M3Al-LDHs (M = Ni, Mg, Co). and it was found that the synergistic effect of transition metal cations on LDH support and Au NCs could enhance the catalytic activity of the oxidation for 1-phenylethanol (Li et al., 2014b).

The design and controllable synthesis of functional nanomaterials based on LDH can have great development and application in the field of adsorption and catalysis. The combination of LDH materials and various other components, such as graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNT), polymer microspheres (Zhu et al., 2017), metal oxides (Shao et al., 2012), and carbon dots (Asiabi et al., 2018) et al., can build multi-level nanocomposites. Meanwhile, the strong coupling between LDH and each component material facilitates the transfer of charges between interfaces and improves the catalytic activity. Wang et al. synthesized a sequence of novel Pdx/rGO@CoAl-LDH nanosheet array-like catalysts through a green lattice atomic-confined in situ reduction way. The as-obtained Pd catalysts showed excellent activity in Heck coupling reactions (Wang et al., 2017c). Carbon dots (CDs), as a kind of new carbon nanomaterials, have been widely used in many fields such as bioimaging, catalysis, biosensing, and luminescence (Xu et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2020). In recent years, CDs have gradually been used as reducing agents for MNPs (Mandani et al., 2015). In addition, the plentiful functional groups on the surface of CDs can also be used as stabilizers to hinder from the aggregation of MNPs and further help to enhance the catalytic activity of materials (Wang and O'Hare, 2012). Iqbal et al. successfully assembled a new class of CDs/Ag@Mg-Al-Ce-LDH catalyst and a synergistic effect between Ag NPs, CDs, and LDH boosted the catalyst's activity for common organic pollutants and 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) (Iqbal et al., 2018).

For the past few years, the mussel chemical method is a relatively common and simple method for surface modification of materials (Saiz-Poseu et al., 2019). Under weakly alkaline conditions, dopamine could deposit a layer of polydopamine (PDA) on the substrate via oxidative self-polymerization. In addition, PDA contains a lot of catechol, amine, imine, etc. functional groups and has strong adhesion, which can be used as a general platform for secondary surface reactions (Cheng et al., 2019). Dou et al. synthesized a new ternary composite material composed of LDH, PDA and polymethyl vinyl ether-malonic anhydride (PMVE-MA) through a combination of a ring-opening reaction and mussel-inspired chemistry (Dou et al., 2019). Recently, our research group grafted poly(N-isopropylacrylamide-co-2,3-epithiopropyl methacrylate) to on the surface of RGO@Fe3O4@PDA through mussel-inspired chemistry. The Au NPs@GFDP nanohybrid material was prepared via in situ reduction using the coordination of episulfide groups with gold (Wang et al., 2017a). Our group also designed and synthesized a catechol-terminated thermally-responsive dihydrophilic diblock copolymer modified graphene oxide (GO) supported palladium-based nanohybrid catalyst by mussel-inspired chemistry and in-situ reduction using the stabilization effect of block copolymers on Pd nanoparticles (Duan et al., 2018).

The easy precipitation and aggregation of LDH sheets restrict their application in various fields. Therefore, the combination of the inorganic matrix and organic stabilizer is expected to produce synergistic effects and new properties. However, there are many researches about the co-precipitation and in-situ polymerization methods to insert polymers between LDH layers, but few reports on the functionalization of LDH surface (Shifrina et al., 2020). Chen et al. reported and prepared a polymer-modified LDH fluorescent probe by using photoinduced surface-induced reversible addition-chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization technology (Chen et al., 2018). It is an effective way to improve the dispersibility of LDH in solution by using different polymer modification. The characteristics of stimulus-responsive polymer materials provide smart response catalytic performance that the conventional catalytic systems can not possess (Guan et al., 2019).

Herein, we constructed a new nanohybrid catalyst (LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs) with carbon dots (CDs) stabilized palladium nanoparticles loaded onto thermo-responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide (PNIPAM) polymer brushes modified 2D LDH nanosheets. Two-dimensional MgAl-LDH sheet was first synthesized via hydrothermal method. Polydopamine was then deposited on the LDH surface through the mussel chemical method to form LDH@PDA. Moreover, the sulfhydryl-terminated PNIPAM polymer was synthesized through RAFT polymerization. Afterwards, the designed PNIPAM was grafted onto LDH@PDA material via Michael addition reaction to obtain LDH@PDA@PNIPAM. The hybrid material displayed better water dispersibility after grafting the polymer. Finally, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanocatalyst was obtained after loading metal Pd on the two-dimensional PNIPAM-modified LDH@PDA sheet with CDs as a stabilizer and reducing agent (Scheme 1 ). The catalytic properties of designed nanocatalyst for different dyes and nitrophenol were systematically investigated. Furthermore, we also evaluated the catalytic effect of the designed catalyst for the Knoevenagel condensation reaction.

Under alkaline conditions, MgAl-LDH was first synthesized via the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 5 mmol of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (1.2820 g) and Al(NO3)3·9H2O (0.6252 g, 1.7 mmol) with a Mg2+/Al3+ molar ratio of 3.0 were first added to 3 mL deionized water. Then, 5 mmol of NaNO3 (0.425 g) in 7 mL deionized water was added, and the resulted mixture was ultrasonicated for a while until the solids were completely dissolved. Under vigorous stirring, the alkaline solution (NaOH, 0.25 M) was gradually added to the above mixture to adjust the pH value to 10.0. Then, the suspension solution was transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, heated at 150 °C for 12 h and cooled to 25 °C. The white emulsion was first washed three times with water, then three times with ethanol, respectively, and then dried under vacuum to obtain the white LDH solid.

The prepared LDH (0.1 g) was dispersed by ultrasound in 40 mL of Tris buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 8.5) to obtain a milky white mixed suspension. Next, 0.05 g of dopamine hydrochloride was added. The reaction suspension was stirred at 25 °C for 1 h. The color of the mixture gradually changed from white to light brown, and finally to dark black. After the reaction, the centrifuged samples were first washed three times with water, then three times with ethanol, and finally dried at 40 °C. The resulting gray-black product was LDH@PDA.

The LDH@PDA@PNIPAM composites were prepared via the Michael addition reaction of sulfhydryl-terminated PNIPAM polymer and dopamine-functionalized LDH. The as-prepared LDH@PDA (50 mg) were added into Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 50 mL) and dispersed by sonication for about 0.5 h. Then, PNIPAM-SH (100 mg) was added into the mixture and stirred for 24 h at 25 °C. The obtained samples were first washed three times with water, then three times with ethanol to remove unreacted polymers. Finally, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM samples were obtained by drying overnight.

First, 20 mg of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM were dispersed in 20 mL deionized water and sonicated for 0.5 h to make it uniformly dispersed. 2.0 mL of PdCl2 ethanol solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added to the above suspension and stirred for 2 h. Then, 0.2 mL CDs (0.01 g ml−1) was added and the reaction suspension was stirred at 25 °C for one day. The product was first washed three times with water, then three times with ethanol, and dried to obtain LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@ Pd/CDs catalyst. We also synthesized LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd hybrid material using NaBH4 as a reducing agent through a similar process as above. In addition, LDH@Pd/CDs, LDH@Pd and Pd/CDs control catalyst samples were also synthesized. The Pd contents of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs.
and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd, LDH@Pd/CDs and LDH@Pd were determined by ICP-AES to be 5.86, 5.49, 6.78 and 5.22 wt%, respectively.

The TEM and FESEM images of different nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 1 . The Pd NPs are successfully loaded on the surface of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM two-dimensional hexahedral hybrid sheet material. As shown in Fig. 1a-c and f, the TEM images show that the average diameter of Pd NPs on the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs sample is about 3.7 nm, and the lattice spacing is about 0.23 nm. Furthermore, we can clearly observe from Fig. S1a that the CDs have an average diameter of 4.25 nm. Simultaneously, the formed Pd particle size was uniform and distributed on the surface of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs. Nevertheless, when NaBH4 is used as the reducing agent for palladium, the formed Pd NPs are unevenly distributed and have a large particle size. The above results reveal that the presence of CDs affects the particle size and distribution of Pd NPs deposited on the surface of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM. It may be that the CDs as a stabilizer and reducing agent have a certain influence on the Pd particle formation. Besides, we further analyzed the surface composition of material by SEM and EDX. The surface of the hybrid material becomes rough after loading Pd NPs (Fig. S1). The EDX element mapping images of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs are shown in Fig. S2. It can be clearly observed that N, S, and Pd elements are uniformly present on the hybrid material. These results clearly confirmed that Pd NPs had been well deposited on the LDH sheet surface.

To investigate the chemical structure of nanohybrids, the FTIR spectra of various samples are shown in Fig. 2 . The peaks at 3460, 1602 and 1370 cm−1 are assigned to the hydroxyl group of the water molecule in the layer as well as physically adsorbed water and NO3 − stretching vibrations (Fig. 2b) (Wang et al., 2017c). The stretching vibration peaks around 800–400 cm−1 are assigned to the metal‑oxygen bond lattice vibration of MgO (Hajibeygi et al., 2018). After modifying LDH with the polydopamine, a new signal peak was observed at 2934 cm−1, which is from the stretching vibration of CH (-CH3 and -CH2-) (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, the intensity of NH vibration peak of the amide group at 1655 cm−1 was significantly enhanced compared with LDH, indicating that the polymer PNIPAM was successfully grafted onto the surface of LDH@PDA (Dou et al., 2019). Owing to the low content of deposited Pd NPs and CDs, the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs showed an IR curve similar to that of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM.

The XRD pattern showed that MgAl-LDH has a typical LDH layered structure (Fig. 3 ). LDH@PDA, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs exhibited diffraction peaks specific to LDH, revealing that the layered structure of LDH has not been destroyed after PDA modification and polymer grafting (Iqbal et al., 2017). The clear diffraction peaks at ~11.2° (003), 23.1° (006), 33.9° (012), 39° (015), 46° (018), 61° (110) and 62.3° (113) are similar to that of MgAl-LDH of the typical hexagonal Co6Al2CO3(OH)16·4H2O (JCPDS 22–0700), demonstrating that the synthesized LDH has a relatively pure phase (Dou and Zhang, 2016). In addition, the sharp diffraction peaks located at 61 and 62.3° owing to the (110) and (113) indicate that the metal cations are well dispersed in the LDH layer. Moreover, the XRD pattern of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs has similar diffraction peaks with LDH (Fig. 3a and d). However, the apparent characteristic peaks of Pd were not observed, which may be due to the low content, ultra-fine size and uniform distribution, and the (111) plane diffraction peak for Pd NPs may also be covered by (015) diffraction plane of LDH. Nevertheless, combined with the TEM analysis, the above results confirmed that Pd NPs have been successfully loaded on the modified LDH support (Li et al., 2020).

The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was further evaluated by TGA. As shown in Fig. 6 , the weight loss of LDH, LDH@PDA, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs between 30–150 °C is the evaporation of residual moisture and organic solvents in the sample. At about 250 °C, the rapid mass loss was ascribed to the removal of water molecules by the de-hydroxylation process. At about 400 °C, the grafted polymer PNIPAM started to disintegrate (Dou et al., 2019). Besides, the mass loss of the original LDH basically stabilized from 400 °C. In contrast, the mass loss of both LDH@PDA, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM continues to increase (Chen et al., 2018). The final weight loss masses of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs and LDH@PDA@PNIPAM are close, indicating that a load of Pd NPs and CDs on the surface of the hybrid material are low. The result above demonstrates that PDA, PNIPAM, Pd and CDs were successfully modified on LDH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst for dyes
ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes including methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), congo red (CR), rhodamine 6G (R6G), and methyl orange (MO) are common toxic organic pollutants in industrial wastewater and soil, which seriously affect the growth of aquatic life and threatens human life and health (Liu et al., 2017). Therefore, these organic dyes were applied as model pollutants for catalytic reduction researches. As displayed in Fig. 7a, Adding 20 μL (0.05 mg mL−1) of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst, the characteristic absorption peak at 662.7 nm of MB solution gradually decreases with increasing reaction time and disappeared completely after 32 s, indicating the MB molecules are successfully reduced to leucomethylene blue (LMB). The reduction process of dyes was completed after 64, 32, 80, and 80 s for LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd, LDH@Pd/CDs, LDH@Pd and Pd/CDs catalysts with the same palladium content, respectively (Fig. 7b, c and Fig. S3). In Fig. S4, the results of the control experiment show that LDH, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM and CDs have lower catalytic activity and hardly react within a few minutes. In the reduction system, since an excess of NaBH4 was used, the reaction conforms to the pseudo-first-order kinetics with ln(Ct/C0) = −kt, where C0 and Ct are denoted as the MB concentrations at the beginning and different times in the course of the reaction, respectively (Huang et al., 2013). As depicted in Fig. 7, the kapp values of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd and LDH@Pd/CDs are 6.37, 3.04, and 4.99 min−1, respectively. Turnover frequency (TOF) is an important indicator of catalytic activity, which is defined as the number of moles of MB reduced per minute per mole of palladium catalyst (Ye et al., 2016). The TOF values of MB catalytic reduction by different catalysts are listed in Table 2 . The TOF value of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst for the catalytic reduction of MB is 545.2 min−1, and its catalytic activity is higher than other catalysts, such as LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd (TOF value: 80.8 min−1) and LDH@Pd/CDs (TOF value: 485.6 min−1). These demonstrated that both the grafting of polymer PNIPAM and the introduction of CDs can improve the catalytic performance of nanocatalysts.

Fig. 8 provides a schematic catalytic reduction mechanism of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@ Pd/CDs for MB. PDA was coated on the surface of LDH through a mussel-inspired method, and the resulted LDH@PDA was used as a support for grafting a hydrophilic PNIPAM polymer to improve the water dispersibility of nanohybrid materials. In addition, CDs have abundant amino and carboxyl groups on their surface, which can act as reducing and stabilizing agents of Pd NPs and have a certain synergistic catalysis with Pd NPs (Iqbal et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 8, LDH have a strong adsorption effect on dye molecules, and can adsorb MB molecules and BH4 − around Pd nanoparticles. At the same time, Pd NPs, which are electron transfer systems, can transfer the electrons provided by BH4 − to the dye. Furthermore, the interfacial interaction between CDs and Pd NPs promotes electron transfer and migration, resulting in synergistic effects, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of nanocomposite catalysts (Bayan and Karak, 2018). Finally, MB molecules are reduced to colorless methylene blue (LMB) and desorbed from the catalyst.

In order to further evaluate the catalytic applicability of the nanohybrid materials, the catalytic reduction of CR, MO, R6G and RhB dyes by LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs was investigated under the same reaction conditions (Fig. S5 and S6). It is revealed that LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanocatalyst exhibits decent catalytic performance for different organic dyes. In addition, the reusability of MB catalytic reduction by using LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs was also investigated. The nanohybrid can be reused at least six consecutive cycles as shown in Fig. S6b, illustrating that LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs has good stability for MB catalytic reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst for 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: We also investigated the catalytic activity of the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@ Pd/CDs hybrid material with the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) as a model reaction using excess NaBH4 as a reducing agent. The absorption of 4-nitrophenol anion at 400 nm decreases with the increasing peak at 300 nm of 4-aminophenol (4-AP), and the reaction quickly completed in 160 s by using LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst (Fig. 9a). The catalytic reductions were completed within 280 and 200 s with LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd and LDH@Pd/CDs. As given in Fig. 9 and Table 3 , LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs displayed higher reaction rate constants (1.09 min−1) and TOF values (101.5 min−1) than other catalysts such as LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd and LDH@Pd/CDs. It is shown that CDs is beneficial to the improvement of catalytic activity, and the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst exhibited better catalytic activity after grafting the polymer. However, the LDH@Pd and Pd/CDs catalysts with Pd NPs were directly supported on LDH, or reduced by CDs without a support have lower catalytic activity with k values of 0.42 and 0.86 min−1(Fig. S7), respectively. We believe that the difference of catalytic activity may be related to the hydrophilicity of the catalyst. It may be that the hybrid material obtained without modification or support has poor dispersibility and Pd NPs are easy to aggregate. Additionally, we also studied the catalytic performance of 4-NP with LDH, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM and CDs hybrid materials as palladium-free catalysts (Fig. S8). The absorption intensity at 400 nm showed little change for a moment, demonstrating that the catalytic activity of these nanocatalysts is poor. In addition, as shown in Fig. S6b, the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanocatalyst can be reused for at least six consecutive cycles. Moreover, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs also displayed excellent catalytic activity for o-NP and m-NP (Fig. S9). We measured the content of each element in the catalyst LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs after cyclic catalysis to study its catalytic stability. As shown in Fig. S23 and Table 1, the element content of the catalyst did not change significantly after six cycles of catalysis and with a small amount of Pd leaching, indicating that the catalyst has good cycle stability. We summarized the catalytic performance of LDH based catalysts in the literature. As shown in Table S1, the TOF value of the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nano-catalyst synthesized is higher than most of the catalysts already reported in the literature, and has excellent catalytic activity.

The high catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs may be due to the increased water dispersion and large specific surface area in the aqueous catalytic system of the catalyst support ((LDH@PDA) after polymer modification, which helps to form uniformly dispersed Pd NPs with smaller size immobilized on brushes-decorated LDH@PDA support. Meanwhile, the polymer-modified nanocatalyst has a higher water dispersibility, which can increase the contacting surface area between the reactants and the active catalytic center of nanocatalyst (Shifrina et al., 2020). Besides, the CDs with organic groups (amino and carboxyl groups) can also reduce Pd2+ to Pd particles, and anchor around them, which can further stabilize Pd NPs and inhibit their aggregation, thereby generating more catalytically active sites and enhancing the catalytic activity (Jin et al., 2016). At the same time, CDs have good electron transfer performance, which is conducive to the electron transfer and synergistic catalysis between CDs and Pd NPs, thereby significantly improving the catalytic activity(Liu et al., 2017). As depicted in Fig. 8, the nanocatalyst transfers the electrons provided by BH4 − to the surrounding nitrophenolate ions and reduces them to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) (Cho et al., 2018). In addition, we monitored the dependence of the catalytic activity of the catalyst on the temperature during the catalytic process from 25 to 45 °C via UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 10a and b, as the temperature rises from 25 to 31 °C, the k value of the catalytic system gradually increases, following the typical Arrhenius equation: lnk app = lnA − (E a/RT), where A is the pre-exponential factor, Ea is the activation energy, and R is the ideal gas constant (Wang et al., 2008; Tang et al., 2015). But, in the catalytic reaction system, as the temperature increases, the k value exhibited an up-down process until the temperature reached 45 °C. As we all know, the PNIPAM is a temperature-sensitive polymer which exhibits hydrophilicity below its low critical dissolution temperature (LCST) and hydrophobic above it. The PNIPAM chain becomes hydrophobic above LCST through conformational adjustment, and shrinks and collapses on the nanocatalyst surface, forming a hydrophobic barrier, which will slow down the contact time between the substrate and the catalytically active center and slow down the reaction rates (Budgin et al., 2012). It can be seen that we can control the catalytic reaction behavior by regulating the temperature of the system for nanocatalyst due to the existence of thermo-responsive polymer chains on the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs for Knoevenagel condensation reaction
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the applicability of the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst, the Knoevenagel condensation reaction between various aldehydes and malononitrile was used as a model reaction to investigate the catalytic performance of the designed nanocatalyst (Gupta et al., 2009; Jia et al., 2018). From the results, it can be seen that a good conversion can be obtained by performing in water at 60 °C for 6 h (Table 4 , entry 1 and Fig. S10). Moreover, the various Knoevenagel condensation reactions of numerous substituted benzyl alcohols and malononitrile were investigated to demonstrate the general applicability of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst. As shown in Table 4 (entries 2–8) and Fig. S11-17, we found that aromatic aldehydes with electron-withdrawing groups (-F, -Cl, -Br, and -NO2) gave relatively considerable conversion rates (Modak et al., 2013). However, the nucleophilic addition reaction of aldehydes containing electron-donating groups (-OCH3) could achieve the highest conversion rate (99%). In fact, the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanocatalysts have excellent conversion rates for the Knoevenagel reaction of benzaldehyde derivatives with electron-withdrawing or electron-donating groups with high reaction conversions (Li et al., 2018).

We also evaluated the Knovenagel condensation reaction of benzaldehyde and malononitrile in water with LDH, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd and LDH@Pd/CDs as catalysts to further reveal the role of different components in the catalyst during the catalytic reaction (Montolio et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2014). As depicted in Table 5 and Fig. S18-21, the conversion rate of the Knovenagel condensation reaction reached 72% with LDH as a catalyst, which is due to the large number of basic sites on the surface of LDH. It can be seen that PNIPAM, Pd NPs and CDs also play a role in the catalysis of the Knoevenagel condensation reaction (Table 5, entries 2–4). The abundant -NH2 on the surface of CDs can also be used as a basic site to facilitate the Knovenagel condensation reaction (Zhang et al., 2019). In addition, we utilized the one-pot Knoevenagel condensation-reduction tandem reaction of 4-nitrobenzaldehyde and malononitrile as a model to investigate the catalytic activity of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs (0.2 mol% Pd) as a bifunctional catalyst (Wang et al., 2019). The first-step Knoevenagel condensation reaction was performed at 60 °C for 6 h, and then the resulting mixture was subjected to one-pot catalytic reduction with NaBH4 solution for 0.5 h (Table 6 and Fig. S22). The results demonstrated that in the reduction step, 2.0 mmol NaBH4 can reduce alkenyl and nitro groups to alkane and amine groups, respectively. After about 30 min, the conversion rate reached 64%. The above analysis indicated that the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst has a good promotion effect on the one-pot cascade reaction(Wang et al., 2017b), while the catalytic performance of the reaction still needs further improvement.

In summary, we designed and synthesized a simple, recoverable, and non-toxic heterogeneous nanohybrid catalyst of LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs. This work also proposed a simple and multifunctional surface modification method for polymer-functionalized two-dimensional LDH sheet nanomaterials based on mussel-inspired chemistry and Michael addition reaction. We successfully grafted the hydrophilic polymer PNIPAM on the surface of LDH@PDA to enhance the water dispersibility of the nanocatalyst's support, and used CDs as the reducing agent to reduce Pd2+ in situ and supported them on PNIPAM modified LDH@PDA to construct a palladium metal nanohybrid catalyst. The different components (LDH and CDs) in the hybrid system can promote the electron transfer and migration, but stabilize and improve the dispersion of Pd NPs. In addition, the prepared catalyst had high catalytic activity for the reduction of nitrophenol and numerous organic dyes. Furthermore, LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs catalyst also showed good conversion and universal applicability for the Knovenagel condensation reaction of malononitrile with various aldehydes. Our work provides a new way for the composite preparation of organic-inorganic hybrid materials by various polymers and inorganic materials with the potential of multi-functional applications.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
We agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to appreciate the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21574017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Materials and characterization, detailed synthesis of carbon dots and PNIPAM-SH, catalytic test; Additional TEM image and EDX spectra of different materials; UV–Vis spectra using LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs for different dyes catalytic reduction, and a comparison for the catalytic performance of different materials in the reduction of MB and 4-NP; 1H NMR spectrum for Knoevenagel condensation reaction catalyzed by different materials. This material is available free of charge via the Internet at https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/applied-clay-science Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clay.2020.105958.
